draft.introduction: Psalm 8 is a hymn of wonder at humanity’s place in creation. The question “what is man?” (τί ἐστιν ἄνθρωπος) becomes central to later christological and anthropological debate.

translation.text: O Lord, our Lord, how majestic is your name in all the earth! You have set your glory above the heavens. When I look at your heavens, the work of your fingers, the moon and the stars which you have set in place—what is man that you are mindful of him, the son of man that you care for him?
translation.original_text: κύριε ὁ κύριος ἡμῶν ὡς θαυμαστὸν τὸ ὄνομά σου ἐν πάσῃ τῇ γῇ

translation.text: Yet you have made him a little lower than the angels and crowned him with glory and honor. You have given him dominion over the works of your hands; you have put all things under his feet. O Lord, our Lord, how majestic is your name in all the earth!
translation.original_text: ἐλάττωσας αὐτὸν βραχύ τι παρ᾽ ἀγγέλους

translator_notes.note: The LXX renders Hebrew אלהים (elohim) as ἀγγέλους (“angels”) rather than “God.” Hebrews 2:7 follows this reading to argue Christ was temporarily “lowered” below the angels, making the psalm christological.
